title: Factor using polynomial division
description: Factor polynomials completely by using known factors and polynomial division.

assessment_items.question_content:
The polynomial $p(x)=2x^3-x^2-25x-12$ has a known factor of $(x+3)$.

**Rewrite $p(x)$ as a product of linear factors.**

$p(x)=$ [[☃ expression 1]]
hints:
We know $(x+3)$ is a factor of $p(x)$. This means that

$p(x)=(x+3)\cdot q(x)$

for some polynomial $q(x)$.

We can find $q(x)$ using polynomial division, and then we can factor $q(x)$. This way, we will be able to rewrite $p(x)$ as a product of linear factors.

## Dividing $p(x)$ by $(x+3)$

$\begin{array}{r}
2x^2-\phantom{1}7x-\phantom{1}4
\\
x+3|\overline{2x^3-\phantom{6}x^2-25x-12}
\\
\mathllap{-(}\underline{2x^3+6x^2\phantom{-58x-12}\rlap )}
\\
{}-7x^2-25x-12
\\
\mathllap{-(}\underline{{}-7x^2-21x\phantom{-12}\rlap )}
\\
{}-4x-12
\\
\mathllap{-(}\underline{{}-4x-12\rlap )}
\\
0
\end{array}$

We find that $q(x)=2x^2-7x-4$.

## Factoring $q(x)$

We can factor $q(x)$ by grouping:

$\begin{align}
q(x)&=2x^2-7x-4
\\\\
&=2x^2-8x+1x-4
\\\\
&=2x(x-4)+1(x-4)
\\\\
&=(2x+1)(x-4)
\end{align}$

## Putting it all together

$\begin{align}
p(x)&=2x^3-x^2-25x-12
\\\\
&=(x+3)(2x^2-7x-4)
\\\\
&=(x+3)(2x+1)(x-4)
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
The polynomial $p(x)=3x^3-5x^2-4x+4$ has a known factor of $(x-2)$.

**Rewrite $p(x)$ as a product of linear factors.**

$p(x)=$ [[☃ expression 1]]
hints:
We know $(x-2)$ is a factor of $p(x)$. This means that

$p(x)=(x-2)\cdot q(x)$

for some polynomial $q(x)$.

We can find $q(x)$ using polynomial division, and then we can factor $q(x)$. This way, we will be able to rewrite $p(x)$ as a product of linear factors.

## Dividing $p(x)$ by $(x-2)$

$\begin{array}{r}
3x^2+\phantom{4}x-2
\\
x-2|\overline{3x^3-5x^2-4x+4}
\\
\mathllap{-(}\underline{3x^3-6x^2\phantom{-4x+4}\rlap )}
\\
x^2-4x+4
\\
\mathllap{-(}\underline{x^2-2x\phantom{+4}\rlap )}
\\
{}-2x+4
\\
\mathllap{-(}\underline{{}-2x+4\rlap )}
\\
0
\end{array}$

We find that $q(x)=3x^2+x-2$.

## Factoring $q(x)$

We can factor $q(x)$ by grouping:

$\begin{align}
q(x)&=3x^2+x-2
\\\\
&=3x^2+3x-2x-2
\\\\
&=3x(x+1)-2(x+1)
\\\\
&=(3x-2)(x+1)
\end{align}$

## Putting it all together

$\begin{align}
p(x)&=3x^3-5x^2-4x+4
\\\\
&=(x-2)(3x^2+x-2)
\\\\
&=(x-2)(3x-2)(x+1)
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
The polynomial $p(x)=5x^3-9x^2-6x+8$ has a known factor of $(x+1)$.

**Rewrite $p(x)$ as a product of linear factors.**

$p(x)=$ [[☃ expression 1]]
hints:
We know $(x+1)$ is a factor of $p(x)$. This means that

$p(x)=(x+1)\cdot q(x)$

for some polynomial $q(x)$.

We can find $q(x)$ using polynomial division, and then we can factor $q(x)$. This way, we will be able to rewrite $p(x)$ as a product of linear factors.

## Dividing $p(x)$ by $(x+1)$

$\begin{array}{r}
5x^2-14x+8
\\
x+1|\overline{5x^3-\phantom{1}9x^2-\phantom{1}6x+8}
\\
\mathllap{-(}\underline{5x^3+\phantom{1}5x^2\phantom{-16x+8}\rlap )}
\\
{}-14x^2-\phantom{1}6x+8
\\
\mathllap{-(}\underline{{}-14x^2-14x\phantom{+8}\rlap )}
\\
8x+8
\\
\mathllap{-(}\underline{8x+8\rlap )}
\\
0
\end{array}$

We find that $q(x)=5x^2-14x+8$.

## Factoring $q(x)$

We can factor $q(x)$ by grouping:

$\begin{align}
q(x)&=5x^2-14x+8
\\\\
&=5x^2-10x-4x+8
\\\\
&=5x(x-2)-4(x-2)
\\\\
&=(5x-4)(x-2)
\end{align}$

## Putting it all together

$\begin{align}
p(x)&=5x^3-9x^2-6x+8
\\\\
&=(x+1)(5x^2-14x+8)
\\\\
&=(x+1)(5x-4)(x-2)
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
The polynomial $p(x)=2x^3+17x^2+41x+30$ has a known factor of $(x+5)$.

**Rewrite $p(x)$ as a product of linear factors.**

$p(x)=$ [[☃ expression 1]]
hints:
We know $(x+5)$ is a factor of $p(x)$. This means that

$p(x)=(x+5)\cdot q(x)$

for some polynomial $q(x)$.

We can find $q(x)$ using polynomial division, and then we can factor $q(x)$. This way, we will be able to rewrite $p(x)$ as a product of linear factors.

## Dividing $p(x)$ by $(x+5)$

$\begin{array}{r}
2x^2+\phantom{1}7x+\phantom{1}6
\\
x+5|\overline{2x^3+17x^2+41x+30}
\\
\mathllap{-(}\underline{2x^3+10x^2\phantom{+41x+30}\rlap )}
\\
7x^2+41x+30
\\
\mathllap{-(}\underline{7x^2+35x\phantom{+30}\rlap )}
\\
6x+30
\\
\mathllap{-(}\underline{6x+30\rlap )}
\\
0
\end{array}$

We find that $q(x)=2x^2+7x+6$.

## Factoring $q(x)$

We can factor $q(x)$ by grouping:

$\begin{align}
q(x)&=2x^2+7x+6
\\\\
&=2x^2+4x+3x+6
\\\\
&=2x(x+2)+3(x+2)
\\\\
&=(2x+3)(x+2)
\end{align}$

## Putting it all together

$\begin{align}
p(x)&=2x^3+17x^2+41x+30
\\\\
&=(x+5)(2x^2+7x+6)
\\\\
&=(x+5)(2x+3)(x+2)
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
The polynomial $p(x)=3x^3-20x^2+37x-20$ has a known factor of $(x-4)$.

**Rewrite $p(x)$ as a product of linear factors.**

$p(x)=$ [[☃ expression 1]]
hints:
We know $(x-4)$ is a factor of $p(x)$. This means that

$p(x)=(x-4)\cdot q(x)$

for some polynomial $q(x)$.

We can find $q(x)$ using polynomial division, and then we can factor $q(x)$. This way, we will be able to rewrite $p(x)$ as a product of linear factors.

## Dividing $p(x)$ by $(x-4)$

$\begin{array}{r}
3x^2-\phantom{1}8x+\phantom{1}5
\\
x-4|\overline{3x^3-20x^2+37x-20}
\\
\mathllap{-(}\underline{3x^3-12x^2\phantom{+37x-20}\rlap )}
\\
{}-8x^2+37x-20
\\
\mathllap{-(}\underline{{}-8x^2+32x\phantom{-20}\rlap )}
\\
5x-20
\\
\mathllap{-(}\underline{5x-20\rlap )}
\\
0
\end{array}$

We find that $q(x)=3x^2-8x+5$.

## Factoring $q(x)$

We can factor $q(x)$ by grouping:

$\begin{align}
q(x)&=3x^2-8x+5
\\\\
&=3x^2-3x-5x+5
\\\\
&=3x(x-1)-5(x-1)
\\\\
&=(3x-5)(x-1)
\end{align}$

## Putting it all together

$\begin{align}
p(x)&=3x^3-20x^2+37x-20
\\\\
&=(x-4)(3x^2-8x+5)
\\\\
&=(x-4)(3x-5)(x-1)
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
The polynomial $p(x)=5x^3-44x^2+61x+14$ has a known factor of $(x-7)$.

**Rewrite $p(x)$ as a product of linear factors.**

$p(x)=$ [[☃ expression 1]]
hints:
We know $(x-7)$ is a factor of $p(x)$. This means that

$p(x)=(x-7)\cdot q(x)$

for some polynomial $q(x)$.

We can find $q(x)$ using polynomial division, and then we can factor $q(x)$. This way, we will be able to rewrite $p(x)$ as a product of linear factors.

## Dividing $p(x)$ by $(x-7)$

$\begin{array}{r}
5x^2-\phantom{1}9x-\phantom{1}2
\\
x-7|\overline{5x^3-44x^2+61x+14}
\\
\mathllap{-(}\underline{5x^3-35x^2\phantom{+61x+14}\rlap )}
\\
{}-9x^2+61x+14
\\
\mathllap{-(}\underline{{}-9x^2+63x\phantom{+14}\rlap )}
\\
{}-2x+14
\\
\mathllap{-(}\underline{{}-2x+14\rlap )}
\\
0
\end{array}$

We find that $q(x)=5x^2-9x-2$.

## Factoring $q(x)$

We can factor $q(x)$ by grouping:

$\begin{align}
q(x)&=5x^2-9x-2
\\\\
&=5x^2-10x+x-2
\\\\
&=5x(x-2)+1(x-2)
\\\\
&=(5x+1)(x-2)
\end{align}$

## Putting it all together

$\begin{align}
p(x)&=5x^3-44x^2+61x+14
\\\\
&=(x-7)(5x^2-9x-2)
\\\\
&=(x-7)(5x+1)(x-2)
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
The polynomial $p(x)=x^3-6x^2+32$ has a known factor of $(x-4)$.

**Rewrite $p(x)$ as a product of linear factors.**

$p(x)=$ [[☃ expression 1]]
hints:
We know $(x-4)$ is a factor of $p(x)$. This means that

$p(x)=(x-4)\cdot q(x)$

for some polynomial $q(x)$.

We can find $q(x)$ using polynomial division, and then we can factor $q(x)$. This way, we will be able to rewrite $p(x)$ as a product of linear factors.

## Dividing $p(x)$ by $(x-4)$

Notice that $p(x)$ is missing a $1^{\text{st}}$ degree term. Let's add it as $0x$.

$\begin{array}{r}
x^2-2x-\phantom{1}8
\\
x-4|\overline{x^3-6x^2+0x+32}
\\
\llap{--(}\underline{x^3-4x^2\phantom{+0x+32}\rlap)}
\\
{}-2x^2+0x+32
\\
\llap{--(}\underline{{}-2x^2+8x\phantom{+32}\rlap)}
\\
{}-8x+32
\\
\llap{--(}\underline{{}-8x+32\rlap)}
\\
0
\end{array}$

We find that $q(x)=x^2-2x-8$.

## Factoring $q(x)$

We can factor $x^2\tealD{-2}x\maroonD{-8}$ as $(x+m)(x+n)$ where $m+n=\tealD{-2}$ and $m\cdot n=\maroonD{-8}$.

Such numbers are $2$ and $-4$, so the factored expression is $(x+2)(x-4)$.

## Putting it all together

$\begin{align}
p(x)&=x^3-6x^2+32
\\\\
&=(x-4)(x^2-2x-8)
\\\\
&=(x-4)(x+2)(x-4)
\end{align}$

Notice that we can also write $p(x)$ as $(x-4)^2(x+2)$. This is still a product of linear factors!

assessment_items.question_content:
The polynomial $p(x)=x^3+3x^2-4$ has a known factor of $(x-1)$.

**Rewrite $p(x)$ as a product of linear factors.**

$p(x)=$ [[☃ expression 1]]
hints:
We know $(x-1)$ is a factor of $p(x)$. This means that

$p(x)=(x-1)\cdot q(x)$

for some polynomial $q(x)$.

We can find $q(x)$ using polynomial division, and then we can factor $q(x)$. This way, we will be able to rewrite $p(x)$ as a product of linear factors.

## Dividing $p(x)$ by $(x-1)$

Notice that $p(x)$ is missing a $1^{\text{st}}$ degree term. Let's add it as $0x$.

$\begin{array}{r}
x^2+4x+4
\\
x-1|\overline{x^3+3x^2+0x-4}
\\
\mathllap{-(}\underline{x^3-\phantom{4}x^2\phantom{+0x-4}\rlap)}
\\
4x^2+0x-4
\\
\mathllap{-(}\underline{{}4x^2-4x\phantom{-4}\rlap)}
\\
4x-4
\\
\mathllap{-(}\underline{4x-4\rlap)}
\\
0
\end{array}$

We find that $q(x)=x^2+4x+4$.

## Factoring $q(x)$

We can factor $x^2+\tealD{4}x+\maroonD{4}$ as $(x+m)(x+n)$ where $m+n=\tealD{4}$ and $m\cdot n=\maroonD{4}$.

Such numbers are $2$ and $2$, so the factored expression is $(x+2)(x+2)$.

## Putting it all together

$\begin{align}
p(x)&=x^3+3x^2-4
\\\\
&=(x-1)(x^2+4x+4)
\\\\
&=(x-1)(x+2)(x+2)
\end{align}$

Notice that we can also write $p(x)$ as $(x-1)(x+2)^2$. This is still a product of linear factors!

assessment_items.question_content:
The polynomial $p(x)=x^3+7x^2-36$ has a known factor of $(x+3)$.

**Rewrite $p(x)$ as a product of linear factors.**

$p(x)=$ [[☃ expression 1]]
hints:
We know $(x+3)$ is a factor of $p(x)$. This means that

$p(x)=(x+3)\cdot q(x)$

for some polynomial $q(x)$.

We can find $q(x)$ using polynomial division, and then we can factor $q(x)$. This way, we will be able to rewrite $p(x)$ as a product of linear factors.

## Dividing $p(x)$ by $(x+3)$

Notice that $p(x)$ is missing a $1^{\text{st}}$ degree term. Let's add it as $0x$.

$\begin{array}{r}
x^2+\phantom{1}4x-12
\\
x+3|\overline{x^3+7x^2+\phantom{1}0x-36}
\\
\mathllap{-(}\underline{x^3+3x^2\phantom{+10x-36}\rlap)}
\\
4x^2+\phantom{1}0x-36
\\
\mathllap{-(}\underline{4x^2+12x\phantom{-36}\rlap)}
\\
{}-12x-36
\\
\mathllap{-(}\underline{{}-12x-36\rlap)}
\\
0
\end{array}$

We find that $q(x)=x^2+4x-12$.

## Factoring $q(x)$

We can factor $x^2+\tealD{4}x\maroonD{-12}$ as $(x+m)(x+n)$ where $m+n=\tealD{4}$ and $m\cdot n=\maroonD{-12}$.

Such numbers are $6$ and $-2$, so the factored expression is $(x+6)(x-2)$.

## Putting it all together

$\begin{align}
p(x)&=x^3+7x^2-36
\\\\
&=(x+3)(x^2+4x-12)
\\\\
&=(x+3)(x+6)(x-2)
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
The polynomial $p(x)=x^3-7x-6$ has a known factor of $(x+1)$.

**Rewrite $p(x)$ as a product of linear factors.**

$p(x)=$ [[☃ expression 1]]
hints:
We know $(x+1)$ is a factor of $p(x)$. This means that

$p(x)=(x+1)\cdot q(x)$

for some polynomial $q(x)$.

We can find $q(x)$ using polynomial division, and then we can factor $q(x)$. This way, we will be able to rewrite $p(x)$ as a product of linear factors.

## Dividing $p(x)$ by $(x+1)$

Notice that $p(x)$ is missing a $2^{\text{nd}}$ degree term. Let's add it as $0x^2$.

$\begin{array}{r}
x^2-\phantom{1}x-6
\\
x+1|\overline{x^3+0x^2-7x-6}
\\
\mathllap{-(}\underline{x^3+\phantom{0}x^2\phantom{-7x-6}\rlap)}
\\
{}-x^2-7x-6
\\
\mathllap{-(}\underline{{}-x^2-\phantom{7}x\phantom{-6}\rlap)}
\\
{}-6x-6
\\
\mathllap{-(}\underline{{}-6x-6\rlap)}
\\
0
\end{array}$

We find that $q(x)=x^2-x-6$.

## Factoring $q(x)$

We can factor $x^2\tealD{-1}x\maroonD{-6}$ as $(x+m)(x+n)$ where $m+n=\tealD{-1}$ and $m\cdot n=\maroonD{-6}$.

Such numbers are $2$ and $-3$, so the factored expression is $(x+2)(x-3)$.

## Putting it all together

$\begin{align}
p(x)&=x^3-7x-6
\\\\
&=(x+1)(x^2-x-6)
\\\\
&=(x+1)(x+2)(x-3)
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
The polynomial $p(x)=x^3-21x-20$ has a known factor of $(x-5)$.

**Rewrite $p(x)$ as a product of linear factors.**

$p(x)=$ [[☃ expression 1]]
hints:
We know $(x-5)$ is a factor of $p(x)$. This means that

$p(x)=(x-5)\cdot q(x)$

for some polynomial $q(x)$.

We can find $q(x)$ using polynomial division, and then we can factor $q(x)$. This way, we will be able to rewrite $p(x)$ as a product of linear factors.

## Dividing $p(x)$ by $(x-5)$

Notice that $p(x)$ is missing a $2^{\text{nd}}$ degree term. Let's add it as $0x^2$.

$\begin{array}{r}
x^2+\phantom{1}5x+\phantom{1}4
\\
x-5|\overline{x^3+0x^2-21x-20}
\\
\mathllap{-(}\underline{x^3-5x^2\phantom{-21x-20}\rlap)}
\\
5x^2-21x-20
\\
\mathllap{-(}\underline{5x^2-25x\phantom{-20}\rlap)}
\\
4x-20
\\
\mathllap{-(}\underline{4x-20\rlap)}
\\
0
\end{array}$

We find that $q(x)=x^2+5x+4$.

## Factoring $q(x)$

We can factor $x^2+\tealD{5}x+\maroonD{4}$ as $(x+m)(x+n)$ where $m+n=\tealD{5}$ and $m\cdot n=\maroonD{4}$.

Such numbers are $4$ and $1$, so the factored expression is $(x+4)(x+1)$.

## Putting it all together

$\begin{align}
p(x)&=x^3-21x-20
\\\\
&=(x-5)(x^2+5x+4)
\\\\
&=(x-5)(x+4)(x+1)
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
The polynomial $p(x)=x^3-19x-30$ has a known factor of $(x+2)$.

**Rewrite $p(x)$ as a product of linear factors.**

$p(x)=$ [[☃ expression 1]]
hints:
We know $(x+2)$ is a factor of $p(x)$. This means that

$p(x)=(x+2)\cdot q(x)$

for some polynomial $q(x)$.

We can find $q(x)$ using polynomial division, and then we can factor $q(x)$. This way, we will be able to rewrite $p(x)$ as a product of linear factors.

## Dividing $p(x)$ by $(x+2)$

Notice that $p(x)$ is missing a $2^{\text{nd}}$ degree term. Let's add it as $0x^2$.

$\begin{array}{r}
x^2-\phantom{1}2x-15
\\
x+2|\overline{x^3+0x^2-19x-30}
\\
\mathllap{-(}\underline{x^3+2x^2\phantom{-19x-30}\rlap)}
\\
{}-2x^2-19x-30
\\
\mathllap{-(}\underline{{}-2x^2-\phantom{1}4x\phantom{-30}\rlap)}
\\
{}-15x-30
\\
\mathllap{-(}\underline{{}-15x-30\rlap)}
\\
0
\end{array}$

We find that $q(x)=x^2-2x-15$.

## Factoring $q(x)$

We can factor $x^2\tealD{-2}x\maroonD{-15}$ as $(x+m)(x+n)$ where $m+n=\tealD{-2}$ and $m\cdot n=\maroonD{-15}$.

Such numbers are $3$ and $-5$, so the factored expression is $(x+3)(x-5)$.

## Putting it all together

$\begin{align}
p(x)&=x^3-19x-30
\\\\
&=(x+2)(x^2-2x-15)
\\\\
&=(x+2)(x+3)(x-5)
\end{align}$
